enrichment.short_description: Open-source radio broadcast automation platform for scheduling, library management, and AM/FM or web playout.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LibreTime is a self-hosted radio broadcast and automation platform used to schedule, manage, and play out audio for community and internet radio stations. It began in 2017 as a community-driven fork of Sourcefabric's Airtime, created after Airtime's open-source development stalled in favor of a paid SaaS offering.

The platform combines a media library, a drag-and-drop scheduling calendar, and a playout engine that can drive traditional AM/FM transmitter chains as well as pure internet radio streams. Shows, sweepers, podcasts, and full-length programs can all be organized and queued for automatic broadcast.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: LibreTime is aimed at community radio stations, campus and internet broadcasters, and nonprofit or volunteer-run stations that need dependable automation without licensing an expensive proprietary system.

features.title: Media library management
features.description: Organize music, sweepers, podcasts, and full-length shows in a searchable library.

features.title: Drag-and-drop scheduling
features.description: Build a broadcast calendar and queue content for automatic playout.

features.title: AM/FM and web playout
features.description: Drive a traditional transmitter chain or stream directly to internet radio listeners.

features.title: Show and podcast automation
features.description: Automatically schedule recurring shows and publish recorded episodes as podcasts.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Install on your own server or infrastructure, with an active GitHub, Discourse, and Matrix community.

pros: Free and open source under AGPLv3
Purpose-built for radio automation, not a generic media server
Handles both terrestrial (AM/FM) and internet radio playout
Active community fork with ongoing development
cons: Requires self-hosting and server administration skills
Setup and configuration are more involved than consumer streaming apps
No official managed/cloud hosting from the project itself

faqs.question: Is LibreTime free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. LibreTime is free, open-source software licensed under AGPLv3 that you self-host.

faqs.question: Is LibreTime the same as Airtime?
faqs.answer: LibreTime is a community fork of Sourcefabric's Airtime, created in 2017 after Airtime's open-source version stopped receiving active development.

faqs.question: Can LibreTime broadcast to an FM transmitter?
faqs.answer: Yes, LibreTime supports AM/FM playout in addition to web/internet radio streaming.

faqs.question: Do I need to host LibreTime myself?
faqs.answer: Yes, LibreTime is self-hosted software; there is no official hosted SaaS version from the project.

use_cases.title: Community radio automation
use_cases.description: Schedule and automate daily programming for a volunteer-run community station.

use_cases.title: Internet radio streaming
use_cases.description: Run a 24/7 internet radio stream with automated playlists and scheduled shows.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: LibreTime is a free, open-source radio automation platform for scheduling and playing out AM/FM and internet radio broadcasts.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source music server that streams local libraries and internet radio to Squeezebox and software players.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lyrion Music Server (LMS) is the community-maintained continuation of Logitech Media Server, the software originally built by Slim Devices to power Squeezebox network audio players. After Logitech ended official development and shut down its mysqueezebox.com cloud service in March 2024, the project was handed to the open-source community and rebranded as Lyrion Music Server starting with version 9.0 in November 2024.

Lyrion streams music from a local library, internet radio stations, and connected streaming services to a wide range of hardware and software players, including legacy Squeezebox devices and modern software players like Squeezelite and piCorePlayer. It can manage multiple synchronized players across different rooms or locations from a single server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Lyrion suits owners of legacy Squeezebox hardware who need continued server support, plus multi-room audio enthusiasts and self-hosters who want a locally controlled alternative to commercial streaming platforms.

features.title: Local library streaming
features.description: Serve your own music collection over the network to any supported player.

features.title: Multi-room synchronization
features.description: Control and synchronize multiple players across different rooms from one server.

features.title: Squeezebox hardware support
features.description: Continues to support legacy Squeezebox network players after Logitech's discontinuation.

features.title: Software player compatibility
features.description: Works with software players such as Squeezelite, piCorePlayer, and SqueezePlay.

features.title: Broad platform installation
features.description: Runs on Windows, macOS, Raspberry Pi, popular NAS platforms (Synology, QNAP, ReadyNAS, OMV), and Docker.

pros: Free and open source, community-maintained
Keeps legacy Squeezebox hardware functional
Wide range of install targets including NAS and Docker
Supports multi-room, synchronized playback
cons: Interface reflects its long legacy rather than a modern redesign
Requires self-hosting and manual setup
Best suited to users already invested in the Squeezebox/LMS ecosystem

faqs.question: Is Lyrion Music Server the same as Logitech Media Server?
faqs.answer: Yes. Lyrion Music Server is the renamed continuation of Logitech Media Server (formerly SlimServer/Squeezebox Server), rebranded after Logitech handed the project to the open-source community in 2024.

faqs.question: Do I need Squeezebox hardware to use Lyrion?
faqs.answer: No. Lyrion also works with software players like Squeezelite, so you can stream without owning legacy Squeezebox hardware.

faqs.question: Is Lyrion free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lyrion Music Server is free, open-source software.

faqs.question: What platforms can run Lyrion Music Server?
faqs.answer: Lyrion runs on Windows, macOS, Raspberry Pi, several NAS platforms, and Docker.

use_cases.title: Legacy Squeezebox support
use_cases.description: Keep older Squeezebox network players working with continued server updates.

use_cases.title: Multi-room home audio
use_cases.description: Stream a personal music library synchronized across multiple rooms and devices.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lyrion Music Server (formerly Logitech Media Server) is a free, open-source server for streaming music to Squeezebox and software players.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source audiophile music player software built specifically for the Raspberry Pi.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: moOde Audio is free, open-source software built exclusively for the Raspberry Pi family of single-board computers, turning inexpensive hardware into an audiophile-grade music streamer. It is developed by and for the DIY audio community, with the source code published on GitHub under GPLv3.

The player supports high-resolution and DSD audio, integrates renderers like Spotify Connect, AirPlay 2, and RoonBridge, and offers customizable audio processing through CamillaDSP with 64-bit volume control. It is controlled entirely through a browser-based interface, so no dedicated app is required on desktop, tablet, or phone.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: moOde is built for DIY audio enthusiasts and audiophiles who want to build a dedicated, high-quality music streamer on Raspberry Pi hardware, with full control over signal processing and output routing.

features.title: High-resolution and DSD playback
features.description: Supports high-res audio formats and native DSD playback for audiophile use.

features.title: Multiroom synchronized audio
features.description: Synchronize playback across multiple moOde installations for whole-home listening.

features.title: CamillaDSP integration
features.description: Customizable digital signal processing with 64-bit volume control.

features.title: Renderer support
features.description: Works with Spotify Connect, AirPlay 2, and RoonBridge for casting from other apps.

features.title: Flexible output and file access
features.description: Connects via USB, HDMI, or I2S outputs, with library access over SMB, NFS, or DLNA.

features.title: Browser-based control
features.description: Fully controlled from any web browser with no dedicated app install required.

pros: Completely free and open source
Purpose-built for high-fidelity audio on affordable Raspberry Pi hardware
No app needed — full browser-based control
Active DIY audio community and frequent updates
cons: Only runs on Raspberry Pi hardware, not general-purpose servers
Setup requires some comfort with SBC hardware and imaging OS files
Best suited to users with an audio-focused use case rather than general media management

faqs.question: What hardware does moOde Audio run on?
faqs.answer: moOde is built specifically for the Raspberry Pi family of single-board computers.

faqs.question: Is moOde Audio free?
faqs.answer: Yes, moOde is free and open-source software licensed under GPLv3.

faqs.question: Do I need an app to control moOde?
faqs.answer: No, moOde is controlled entirely through a web browser on any device.

faqs.question: Does moOde support Spotify or AirPlay?
faqs.answer: Yes, moOde integrates with renderers including Spotify Connect, AirPlay 2, and RoonBridge.

use_cases.title: DIY audiophile streamer
use_cases.description: Turn a Raspberry Pi and DAC into a dedicated high-resolution music streamer.

use_cases.title: Whole-home multiroom audio
use_cases.description: Synchronize multiple Raspberry Pi units running moOde for multiroom playback.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: moOde Audio is free, open-source audiophile music player software built exclusively for the Raspberry Pi, controlled entirely from a browser.

enrichment.short_description: Extensible, open-source music server written in Python that plays local files, streaming services, and radio via extensions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mopidy is an extensible music server written in Python, first released in December 2009. Its core is deliberately minimal; nearly all of its capability — local file playback, Spotify, SoundCloud, internet radio, and more — is added through a large ecosystem of community-built extensions.

Because Mopidy exposes an MPD-compatible interface and its own HTTP/JSON API, it can be controlled by a wide range of existing MPD clients as well as dedicated Mopidy web and mobile frontends, making it a flexible backbone for headless music setups such as the Raspberry Pi-based Pi MusicBox distribution.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Mopidy appeals to developers and tinkerers who want a hackable, extension-driven music server, and to self-hosters running a headless music player (e.g., on a Raspberry Pi) that they control with a separate client app.

features.title: Extension architecture
features.description: Add music sources, frontends, and features through a large library of community extensions.

features.title: MPD-compatible protocol
features.description: Can be controlled by existing MPD client applications thanks to protocol compatibility.

features.title: HTTP/JSON API
features.description: Exposes a web API for building custom or web-based clients.

features.title: Multiple backend sources
features.description: Extensions add support for local files, Spotify, SoundCloud, internet radio, and more.

features.title: Headless server operation
features.description: Runs as a background server, ideal for Raspberry Pi and other always-on devices.

pros: Free and open source
Highly extensible via a large plugin ecosystem
Compatible with existing MPD client software
Lightweight enough for Raspberry Pi and similar devices
cons: Core install has limited functionality without extensions
Configuration and extension setup require technical familiarity
No official mobile or desktop app is bundled — relies on third-party clients

faqs.question: Is Mopidy free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mopidy is free, open-source software.

faqs.question: Does Mopidy support Spotify?
faqs.answer: Spotify and other streaming sources are supported through community-built Mopidy extensions, not the core install.

faqs.question: What platforms does Mopidy run on?
faqs.answer: Mopidy can be installed on Linux, macOS, and Raspberry Pi.

faqs.question: Can I use an existing MPD client with Mopidy?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mopidy implements an MPD-compatible protocol so many existing MPD clients work with it.

use_cases.title: Headless home music server
use_cases.description: Run Mopidy on a Raspberry Pi as an always-on server controlled by a phone or web client.

use_cases.title: Custom music client development
use_cases.description: Build a custom frontend against Mopidy's HTTP/JSON API or extension system.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mopidy is a free, open-source, extensible music server written in Python that plays local and streamed music via community extensions.

enrichment.short_description: Music Player Daemon is a flexible, server-side music player controlled remotely by third-party client applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Music Player Daemon (MPD) is a flexible, server-side application for playing music, first released in 2003. It runs as a background daemon on a server or single-board computer and exposes a network protocol that client applications connect to for playback control, rather than providing a built-in graphical interface itself.

MPD supports a wide range of audio formats through plugins and libraries, and it has spawned a large ecosystem of third-party clients across desktop, web, and mobile (including tools like myMPD and various iOS clients) that connect to a running MPD server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: MPD is aimed at technical users and self-hosters who want a lightweight, headless music playback backend that they can control with any of the many available third-party clients, rather than a single all-in-one app.

features.title: Client-server architecture
features.description: The daemon runs on a server and is controlled remotely by any compatible client over its network protocol.

features.title: Plugin-based format support
features.description: Supports numerous audio formats and outputs through plugins and libraries.

features.title: Large client ecosystem
features.description: A wide range of third-party desktop, web, and mobile clients (e.g., myMPD, various iOS apps) can control MPD.

features.title: Lightweight, headless operation
features.description: Runs efficiently on low-power hardware such as Raspberry Pi and NAS devices.

pros: Free and open source with a long track record of stability
Very lightweight, suitable for low-power hardware
Large ecosystem of compatible client apps
Well-documented network protocol for building custom clients
cons: No official graphical interface — requires a separate client to use
Setup is more technical than an all-in-one media player
Feature set is intentionally minimal compared to full media servers

faqs.question: Is MPD free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Music Player Daemon is free, open-source software.

faqs.question: Does MPD have its own app?
faqs.answer: No, MPD is a headless daemon; you connect to it using one of many third-party client applications.

faqs.question: What can control MPD?
faqs.answer: Any client that implements the MPD protocol, including desktop, web, and mobile apps such as myMPD.

use_cases.title: Headless music server
use_cases.description: Run MPD on a Raspberry Pi, NAS, or home server and control playback from any MPD-compatible client.

use_cases.title: Custom client development
use_cases.description: Build a custom player interface against MPD's documented network protocol.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: MPD (Music Player Daemon) is a free, open-source server-side music player controlled remotely by a wide ecosystem of client apps.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source personal music streaming server with offline sync and dedicated iOS and Android apps.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: mStream is a free, open-source music streaming server that lets you access your own music collection from anywhere, with offline syncing for mobile listening. It functions as a self-hosted alternative to commercial streaming services, keeping your library and listening data under your own control.
The server supports gapless playback, a Milkdrop-based visualizer, playlist sharing, and drag-and-drop file uploads, and it streams uncompressed FLAC for users who want lossless audio quality.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: mStream suits privacy-conscious listeners and audiophiles who want to self-host their music collection and stream it to mobile apps without ads, algorithmic tracking, or subscription fees.

features.title: Cross-platform streaming
features.description: Runs on any operating system and streams to a web browser or dedicated mobile apps.

features.title: Offline sync
features.description: Sync tracks to iOS and Android apps for offline playback.

features.title: Lossless FLAC streaming
features.description: Streams uncompressed FLAC for audiophile-quality playback.

features.title: Gapless playback
features.description: Plays consecutive tracks without gaps for a continuous listening experience.

features.title: Playlist sharing
features.description: Create and share playlists with other users.

features.title: Drag-and-drop uploads
features.description: Add music to the library through a simple drag-and-drop upload interface.

pros: Free and open source
Dedicated native iOS and Android apps
Offline sync for mobile listening
Supports lossless FLAC streaming
cons: Requires self-hosting and server setup
Smaller community and slower update cadence than larger media server projects
Interface is more basic than commercial streaming apps

faqs.question: Is mStream free?
faqs.answer: Yes, mStream is free, open-source software available on GitHub.

faqs.question: Does mStream have mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, mStream offers dedicated iOS and Android apps with offline sync support.

faqs.question: Can mStream stream lossless audio?
faqs.answer: Yes, mStream supports streaming uncompressed FLAC files.

use_cases.title: Personal music streaming
use_cases.description: Self-host a personal music library and stream it to any device over the web.

use_cases.title: Offline mobile listening
use_cases.description: Sync tracks to the mStream mobile app for offline playback while traveling.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: mStream is a free, open-source music streaming server with offline sync and dedicated iOS and Android apps for lossless FLAC playback.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted tool that monitors your listening activity across many music sources and scrobbles it to Last.fm, ListenBrainz, and more.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: multi-scrobbler is a self-hosted, Docker-friendly application that monitors music listening activity across many sources and forwards ('scrobbles') it to one or more tracking services. It bridges the gap left by services that no longer natively scrobble everywhere you listen.

It supports a long list of sources — including Spotify, Plex, Jellyfin, YouTube Music, Kodi, Mopidy, VLC, and Subsonic-compatible servers like Navidrome and Airsonic — and can send scrobbles to destinations including Last.fm, ListenBrainz, Libre.fm, Maloja, Koito, and Discord. Failed scrobbles are queued and retried automatically, and a built-in web interface exposes stats, logs, and runtime configuration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: multi-scrobbler is built for self-hosters and music enthusiasts who listen across multiple apps and services and want a single, centralized, privacy-respecting scrobbling history — including those tracking listening for friends or family members.

features.title: Broad source support
features.description: Monitors Spotify, Plex, Jellyfin, YouTube Music, Kodi, Mopidy, VLC, and Subsonic-compatible servers, among 20+ sources.

features.title: Multiple scrobble destinations
features.description: Sends scrobbles to Last.fm, ListenBrainz, Libre.fm, Maloja, Koito, and Discord.

features.title: MusicBrainz data standardization
features.description: Uses the MusicBrainz database to standardize track and artist metadata.

features.title: Multi-user support
features.description: Track scrobbles for multiple people from a single deployment.

features.title: Retry queue
features.description: Automatically queues and retries scrobbles that fail due to network issues.

features.title: Web dashboard
features.description: View statistics, logs, and configuration from a built-in web interface.

pros: Free and open source under the MIT license
Wide compatibility with music players and streaming sources
Docker support for easy self-hosted deployment
Automatic retry for failed scrobbles improves reliability
cons: Requires self-hosting (Docker or Node.js) rather than a hosted option
Configuration of multiple sources/clients can take time to set up
Primarily a background/utility tool rather than a full media player

faqs.question: Is multi-scrobbler free?
faqs.answer: Yes, multi-scrobbler is free and open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: Which services can multi-scrobbler scrobble to?
faqs.answer: It can send scrobbles to Last.fm, ListenBrainz, Libre.fm, Maloja, Koito, and Discord.

faqs.question: Can it track multiple people's listening?
faqs.answer: Yes, multi-scrobbler supports multi-user setups so you can scrobble for friends or family from one instance.

faqs.question: How is multi-scrobbler deployed?
faqs.answer: It runs as a Docker container (x86/ARM) or as a local Node.js application.

use_cases.title: Consolidated listening history
use_cases.description: Merge listening activity from Spotify, Plex, Jellyfin, and other apps into one scrobbling history.

use_cases.title: Family scrobbling
use_cases.description: Track scrobbles for multiple household members from a single self-hosted instance.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: multi-scrobbler is a free, open-source tool that scrobbles listening activity from Spotify, Plex, Jellyfin, and more to Last.fm and ListenBrainz.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source terminal-based music player, library manager, and streaming server for large local collections.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: musikcube is a fully functional, terminal-based music player, library manager, and streaming audio server that runs natively on Windows, macOS, and Linux, including on a Raspberry Pi with a custom DAC. It is built around three components: the core musikcube terminal player, the musikdroid Android companion app, and musikcore, a C++ library developers can build on.

It handles file scanning, tag indexing, gapless and crossfading playback, play queue management, and playlist CRUD operations, with an extensible plugin architecture and support for high-quality audio output up to 24-bit/192kHz. The project is designed to handle very large libraries, with reported support for collections exceeding 250,000 tracks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: musikcube appeals to command-line-oriented music enthusiasts, home server hobbyists who want a lightweight streaming backend, and developers building on the musikcore library, including Raspberry Pi tinkerers assembling custom audio setups.

features.title: Terminal-based interface
features.description: A full-featured text UI for browsing, searching, and playing music from the command line.

features.title: Streaming server mode
features.description: Runs as a headless server that streams audio to remote clients.

features.title: Android companion app
features.description: musikdroid connects to a musikcube server for mobile playback and control.

features.title: Gapless and crossfading playback
features.description: Supports seamless track transitions for continuous listening.

features.title: Extensible plugin architecture
features.description: Add functionality through plugins built on the musikcore C++ library.

features.title: High-resolution audio output
features.description: Supports audio output up to 24-bit/192kHz.

pros: Free and open source
Very lightweight, runs well on Raspberry Pi hardware
Handles very large music libraries efficiently
Cross-platform: Windows, macOS, and Linux
cons: Terminal-based interface has a learning curve for non-technical users
No official iOS app, only Android (musikdroid)
Smaller community than mainstream media server projects

faqs.question: Is musikcube free?
faqs.answer: Yes, musikcube is free and open-source software.

faqs.question: Does musikcube have a graphical interface?
faqs.answer: musikcube's core interface is terminal-based, though it also functions as a streaming server for remote clients like the musikdroid Android app.

faqs.question: What platforms does musikcube support?
faqs.answer: musikcube runs natively on Windows, macOS, and Linux, and performs well on a Raspberry Pi with a custom DAC.

use_cases.title: Command-line music playback
use_cases.description: Manage and play a large local music library entirely from the terminal.

use_cases.title: Lightweight home streaming server
use_cases.description: Run musikcube as a headless server on a Raspberry Pi and stream to the musikdroid mobile app.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: musikcube is a free, open-source terminal-based music player, library manager, and streaming server for Windows, macOS, and Linux.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, Rust-based podcast manager with cross-device sync, YouTube channel subscriptions, and multi-user support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: PinePods is a self-hosted podcast management platform built in Rust that emphasizes performance and full data ownership. It provides a complete podcast ecosystem — playback position, settings, subscriptions, and themes all sync across every device you use.

Beyond standard podcast subscriptions, PinePods can subscribe to YouTube channels and deliver their audio as podcast-style downloads. It includes smart playlists with synchronized filtering, podcast discovery through the Podcast Index and iTunes search, multi-user support with separate libraries per family member, and rich episode metadata including chapters, transcripts, people information, and funding links. It also supports a built-in gpodder server or external sync services like Podsync and Nextcloud.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: PinePods is built for self-hosters and privacy-focused podcast listeners who want full control of their subscriptions and listening data across phone, desktop, web, and terminal clients, without relying on a commercial podcast app.

features.title: Cross-device sync
features.description: Playback position, subscriptions, settings, and themes sync instantly across every connected device.

features.title: YouTube channel subscriptions
features.description: Subscribe to YouTube channels and receive audio-only downloads formatted as podcast episodes.

features.title: Smart playlists
features.description: Build customizable, filterable playlists that stay in sync across devices.

features.title: Podcast discovery search
features.description: Find new shows using integrated Podcast Index and iTunes search.

features.title: Multi-user libraries
features.description: Separate podcast libraries for each family member or user account.

features.title: gpodder-compatible sync
features.description: Use the built-in gpodder server or connect external services like Podsync or Nextcloud.

pros: Completely free and open source
Broad client coverage: iOS, Android, web, desktop, and CLI
Full cross-device sync included at no cost
YouTube channel-to-podcast conversion built in
cons: Requires self-hosting a Rust-based server
Newer project with a smaller community than established podcast apps
Initial setup and Docker configuration require some technical comfort

faqs.question: Is PinePods free?
faqs.answer: Yes, PinePods is completely free and open-source software.

faqs.question: Can PinePods sync across multiple devices?
faqs.answer: Yes, playback position, subscriptions, settings, and themes all sync across every device you connect.

faqs.question: Does PinePods support YouTube?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can subscribe to YouTube channels and receive their audio as podcast-style downloads.

faqs.question: What clients are available for PinePods?
faqs.answer: PinePods offers iOS and Android apps, a web app, desktop apps for Windows/macOS/Linux, and a CLI client called Firewood.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted podcast library
use_cases.description: Host and manage all your podcast subscriptions with full data ownership.

use_cases.title: Family podcast management
use_cases.description: Give each household member their own synced podcast library on one server.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: PinePods is a free, open-source, self-hosted podcast manager with cross-device sync, YouTube subscriptions, and multi-user support.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source music streaming server with a modern interface, described by its creators as a Spotify-like experience for your own files.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Swing Music is a self-hosted music streaming server designed to give it a modern, polished interface comparable to commercial streaming apps while keeping your library fully under your own control. It is open source under AGPLv3 and can be installed via a command-line script on Linux and Apple Silicon Macs, via Docker, or as a portable binary on Linux, Windows, and Mac.

Core features include folder-based browsing, typo-tolerant fuzzy search, automatic duplicate detection that favors higher-bitrate files, and collections for grouping albums and artists. Listening statistics are tracked for all users, while advanced discovery features such as personalized mixes are offered as a premium option supported through a one-time payment or GitHub sponsorship.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Swing Music suits self-hosters who want a visually modern alternative to commercial streaming apps for their own local music files, without giving up ownership of their library.

features.title: Modern streaming interface
features.description: A polished, browser-based UI designed to feel like a commercial streaming app.

features.title: Folder-based browsing
features.description: Navigate your library by directory structure in addition to standard views.

features.title: Fuzzy search
features.description: Typo-tolerant search helps you find tracks even with imperfect spelling.

features.title: Duplicate detection
features.description: Automatically identifies duplicate tracks and prefers the higher-bitrate version.

features.title: Collections
features.description: Group albums, artists, and other library items into custom collections.

features.title: Listening statistics
features.description: Tracks listening activity and surfaces basic insights for your library.

pros: Free, open-source core with a modern, clean interface
Runs on Linux, Windows, and Mac, plus Docker
Smart duplicate detection saves storage and library cleanliness
Active development supported by GitHub sponsorships
cons: Advanced discovery/mix features are gated behind a paid premium tier
No dedicated iOS app was found at time of writing (Android client available)
Newer project with a smaller ecosystem than established self-hosted media servers

faqs.question: Is Swing Music free?
faqs.answer: The core server is free and open source. A premium tier unlocking features like personalized mixes is available through a one-time payment or GitHub sponsorship.

faqs.question: What platforms can run Swing Music?
faqs.answer: Swing Music installs on Linux and Apple Silicon Macs via script, or as a portable binary on Linux, Windows, and Mac, plus Docker.

faqs.question: Does Swing Music detect duplicate tracks?
faqs.answer: Yes, it automatically detects duplicates and keeps the higher-bitrate version.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted personal streaming
use_cases.description: Stream your own music library through a modern, Spotify-like web interface.
use_cases.audience: Self-hosters wanting a polished local music UI

use_cases.title: Library cleanup and organization
use_cases.description: Use collections and duplicate detection to tidy up a large personal music collection.
use_cases.audience: Music collectors with large local libraries

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Swing Music is an open-source, self-hosted music streaming server with a modern interface, folder browsing, and smart duplicate detection.
